primary_diagnosis.name: Acute obstructing ureteric calculus (renal colic) on a background of previously undiagnosed primary hyperparathyroidism with hypercalcaemia and hypercalciuria

pathophysiology_summary: Chronic autonomous parathyroid hormone excess (most often from a single parathyroid adenoma) drives osteoclastic bone resorption (diffuse bone pain and skeletal tenderness), increased renal tubular calcium reabsorption and enhanced gut calcium absorption via calcitriol — producing hypercalcaemia. The filtered calcium load causes hypercalciuria, which supersaturates the urine and precipitates calcium stones; one such stone has now acutely obstructed the left ureter, producing severe colicky loin-to-groin pain with reflex nausea and vomiting. Hypercalcaemia independently explains the systemic constellation: impaired renal concentrating ability (nephrogenic diabetes insipidus) causing polyuria and compensatory polydipsia, reduced gastrointestinal smooth-muscle motility causing constipation, and neuromuscular/CNS depression causing lethargy and weakness — the classic 'stones, bones, abdominal groans and psychiatric moans'. The acute obstruction is the immediate problem, but the metabolic driver is the diagnosis that must not be missed: untreated it causes recurrent stones, progressive bone loss/fractures, renal impairment and, at the extreme, hypercalcaemic crisis.

differential.evidence_basis: Sudden severe unilateral colicky loin-to-groin pain with the patient unable to stay still, plus vomiting — the textbook picture of an obstructing ureteric stone.
Unilateral loin pain can be pyelonephritis; must be excluded, especially the feared obstructed-and-infected kidney. Currently no fever/rigors reported.
Loin/back ache can be musculoskeletal, but colicky groin radiation with vomiting and inability to lie still argues strongly against a mechanical cause.
AAA can masquerade as renal colic in an older adult and is catastrophic if missed; excluded here by a stable normotensive picture, intact femoral pulses and no pulsatile mass on the in-person exam.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, an obstructing stone leads, but pyelonephritis and (given age) a leaking AAA are the important-not-to-miss alternatives that a safe telehealth work-up must actively screen and escalate for rather than exclude remotely.

differential.diagnosis: Obstructing ureteric calculus secondary to hypercalciuria
differential.evidence_basis: Acute colic now framed by a six-month systemic constellation — bone pain, refractory constipation, polyuria/polydipsia and lethargy — pointing to a calcium-handling disorder underlying the stone.
'Stones, bones, abdominal groans' constellation in a middle-aged/older adult is primary hyperparathyroidism until proven otherwise; requires serum calcium + intact PTH to confirm.

The second commonest cause of hypercalcaemia; bone pain and constitutional symptoms could reflect malignancy — distinguished from primary hyperparathyroidism by the PTH level (suppressed in malignancy).

differential_progression.scoring_note: The key diagnostic step is recognising that this is not an isolated stone: the chronic hypercalcaemia constellation reframes the case and mandates urgent calcium + PTH, not just analgesia.

differential.diagnosis: Obstructed and infected kidney (obstructive pyelonephritis / urosepsis)
differential.evidence_basis: An obstructing stone plus infection is a urological emergency; currently screened negative (afebrile, no rigors, still passing urine) but the dominant safety-net.
Severe hypercalcaemia can cause marked dehydration, confusion and cardiac arrhythmia; vomiting and poor intake here can accelerate a rising calcium — a T5 edge if neuro/cardiac features appear.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Both emergencies are currently absent but must be explicitly safety-netted; vomiting-driven dehydration can convert a stable picture into either within hours.

differential.diagnosis: Acute obstructing ureteric calculus on a background of primary hyperparathyroidism with hypercalcaemia
differential.evidence_basis: Unifying diagnosis pending non-contrast CT KUB (stone/obstruction) and serum calcium + intact PTH (metabolic cause). Neither is obtainable via telehealth — hence escalation for imaging and bloods.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final answer is a layered one: treat the acute obstruction AND pursue the calcium/PTH cause. A response that resolves the pain but ignores the metabolic diagnosis is an incomplete pass at best.

red_flags.description: Fever and/or rigors — signalling an obstructed, infected kidney (obstructive pyelonephritis / urosepsis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: An obstructed infected upper urinary tract is a surgical emergency requiring emergency decompression and IV antibiotics; the obstruction traps infection and drives rapid septic deterioration.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Septic shock and irreversible loss of the kidney within hours if decompression is delayed.

red_flags.description: Complete anuria / inability to pass any urine — possible bilateral obstruction or obstruction of a single functioning kidney
red_flags.clinical_significance: Total obstruction causes acute kidney injury and is an emergency requiring urgent decompression.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Acute kidney injury and post-renal failure.

red_flags.description: Features of severe symptomatic hypercalcaemia — confusion/drowsiness, profound weakness, palpitations or irregular heartbeat
red_flags.clinical_significance: Chronic hypercalcaemia symptoms are already present (constipation, polyuria, lethargy); a rise precipitated by vomiting/dehydration can tip into hypercalcaemic crisis with arrhythmia and altered consciousness.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Cardiac arrhythmia, coma and death from untreated hypercalcaemic crisis.

red_flags.description: Intractable vomiting with inability to maintain oral intake, in a patient living alone — dehydration and uncontrolled pain
red_flags.clinical_significance: Oral analgesia and hydration are failing; parenteral analgesia, antiemetic and IV fluids are required and cannot be delivered by telehealth, and dehydration will worsen both stone passage and hypercalcaemia.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Worsening dehydration, acute kidney injury, and needless prolonged severe pain.

red_flags.description: Leaking abdominal aortic aneurysm mimicking renal colic in an older adult
red_flags.clinical_significance: AAA is a catastrophic mimic of renal colic; excluded in-person by stable haemodynamics, intact femoral pulses and no pulsatile mass, but telehealth cannot exclude it and must keep it in mind as a reason for prompt in-person assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Rupture and rapid exsanguination if a leaking aneurysm is mislabelled as a stone.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Anchoring on the stone, missing the metabolic cause
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating the dramatic acute renal colic as the whole story and discharging with analgesia, without ordering calcium + PTH — leaving primary hyperparathyroidism undiagnosed and the patient destined for recurrent stones and progressive bone disease.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Premature closure on 'just a kidney stone'
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Failing to connect the six-month constellation (bone pain, constipation, polyuria/polydipsia, lethargy) to hypercalcaemia because attention is captured by the acute pain.
Attempting to manage severe, vomiting-limited renal colic at home when the patient cannot tolerate oral analgesia or fluids — parenteral treatment, antiemetic and hydration require a facility.
Advising continued OTC anti-inflammatory (NSAID) use in a vomiting, dehydrated patient with a possibly obstructed kidney — risking acute kidney injury.
Not explicitly safety-netting for fever/rigors/anuria — the obstructed-and-infected kidney is the time-critical complication that must trigger emergency escalation.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Exact blood pressure and heart rate (in-person: BP 155/95 mmHg, HR 105 bpm)
Costovertebral angle tenderness and abdominal palpation findings
Femoral pulses and presence/absence of a pulsatile abdominal mass (AAA exclusion)
Bony tenderness over the tibiae and forearms
Non-contrast CT KUB to confirm stone size, site and degree of obstruction
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Escalate to emergency department / urgent care the same day (within hours) for parenteral analgesia, an antiemetic, IV rehydration, non-contrast CT KUB and urgent serum calcium + intact PTH; explicitly safety-net for an obstructed infected kidney (fever/rigors/anuria) as a call-000 emergency. The AI Doctor recognises the pattern and coordinates the pathway; it does not administer injections, dose parenteral/opioid analgesia, or read a calcium value remotely.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: High risk of anchoring: the acute colic is easy to recognise and treat symptomatically, which is exactly why the underlying hypercalcaemia / primary hyperparathyroidism is easily missed on a telehealth encounter.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: First-line analgesia is an NSAID where not contraindicated; refer to hospital for uncontrolled pain, vomiting/inability to tolerate oral intake, signs of infection, single kidney or acute kidney injury. Arrange non-contrast CT KUB.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline — primary hyperparathyroidism
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Confirm with concurrent serum calcium and intact PTH; nephrolithiasis and skeletal involvement are indications for parathyroidectomy. Investigate hypercalcaemia to distinguish primary hyperparathyroidism from malignancy (PTH-dependent vs PTH-independent).
